relationshipToCourse: Exam-style practice source; not confirmed against Vinci's June 2024 manual
pilotNote: Complete: all 91 items from the source PDF have been processed (the initial 25-item pilot validated the schema; items 26-91 were added in a follow-up pass using the identical schema and rules). See docs/CONTENT_INTAKE_BENCHMARK.md for how this source's Tier 3 status affects what it can be used for. No item in this file may be marked verified — per the benchmark doc's rule, verification requires tracing to Vinci's actual materials or an official source, neither of which this file is.

items.originalQuestion: the term "pintle" and "gudgeon" are associated with the
items.cleanedQuestion: The terms "pintle" and "gudgeon" are associated with which piece of lifeboat equipment?
items.cleanedAnswer: The rudder.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Clear vocabulary item; no ambiguity.

items.originalQuestion: When using the term limber system one is referring to a
items.originalAnswer: drainage system holes in foors allows water to fow thru compartments
items.cleanedQuestion: When using the term "limber system," what is being referred to?
items.cleanedAnswer: A drainage system: holes in the floors (limber holes) that let water flow through compartments toward the bilge/drain.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: No subtopic bullet named 'limber system' in the given manual TOC; mapped to the closest construction-related subtopic (Interior Construction). OCR errors 'foors'/'fow' corrected to 'floors'/'flow'.

items.originalQuestion: The standard magnetic compass heading differs from the true heading by
items.cleanedQuestion: The standard magnetic compass heading differs from the true heading by what amount/factor?
items.cleanedAnswer: Compass error (the combined effect of variation and deviation).
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Exact duplicate of source item 37 (outside this 25-item pilot). Also closely related to variation and deviation items later in the source — good candidate for a compare/contrast question distinguishing compass error vs. variation vs. deviation once verified.

items.originalQuestion: After an engine is started you should
items.originalAnswer: check operating pressures and temperatures, and check for leaks
items.cleanedQuestion: After starting a lifeboat engine, what should you do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Check operating pressures and temperatures, and check for leaks.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Clear procedural item; no ambiguity.

items.originalQuestion: A certifed lifeboatman assigned to command the lifeboat must
items.originalAnswer: have a list of the persons assigned to the lfieboat
items.cleanedQuestion: A certified lifeboatman assigned to command a lifeboat must do what?
items.cleanedAnswer: Have a list of the persons assigned to that lifeboat.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typos 'certifed'/'lfieboat' corrected. Lifeboat-specific command duty could alternatively sit under Chapter 2's lifeboat-crew content instead of Chapter 1.

items.originalQuestion: The survival crafts engine is fueled with
items.cleanedQuestion: The survival craft's engine is fueled with what?
items.cleanedAnswer: Diesel oil.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Near-duplicate of source item 44 (outside this 25-item pilot).

items.originalQuestion: When inspecting a survival craft, you should check to make sure
items.originalAnswer: hydraulic pressure is within specifed range
items.cleanedQuestion: When inspecting a survival craft, what should you check?
items.cleanedAnswer: That hydraulic pressure is within the specified range.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Not clear whether this refers to engine hydraulics, steering hydraulics, or a hydraulic davit/release system — any of the three is plausible. Mapped to Propulsion Systems as the best single guess.

items.originalQuestion: When a sea anchor is used in landing stern frst in a heavy surf, sternway is checked by
items.originalAnswer: slacking the tripping line and towing the sea anchor by holding the line
items.cleanedQuestion: When a sea anchor is used to land stern-first in heavy surf, how is sternway (backward motion) checked/controlled?
items.cleanedAnswer: By slacking the tripping line and towing the sea anchor, holding it by the line.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'frst' corrected to 'first'.

items.originalQuestion: Your vessel is equipped with totally enclosed lifeboats. Which is true when the boat is enveloped in fames?
items.originalAnswer: An air tank will provide about ten minutes of air for the survivors and the engine
items.cleanedQuestion: Your vessel is equipped with totally enclosed lifeboats. Which statement is true when the boat is enveloped in flames?
items.cleanedAnswer: An air tank provides about ten minutes of air for the survivors and the engine while passing through flames.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'fames' corrected to 'flames'. Good misconception-trap material: a student might assume a totally enclosed lifeboat offers indefinite fire protection, when the real answer specifies a firm ~10-minute air supply limit. Fire-protection/air-supply systems aren't an explicitly named subtopic in the given TOC excerpt; mapped to the closest equipment bucket.

items.originalQuestion: Most lifeboats are equipped with
items.cleanedQuestion: Most lifeboats are equipped with what type of rudder?
items.cleanedAnswer: Unbalanced rudders.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Clear vocabulary/fact item.

items.originalQuestion: Your vessel has lifeboats on both sides. Lifeboat #2 is located
items.originalAnswer: forwards of lifeboat #4 on the port side
items.cleanedQuestion: Your vessel has lifeboats on both sides, numbered per standard convention. Where is Lifeboat #2 located?
items.cleanedAnswer: Forward of Lifeboat #4, on the port side.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Standard even-numbers-port/odd-numbers-starboard, bow-to-stern numbering convention; should be double-checked against the manual's exact numbering rule before use.

items.originalQuestion: Stretchers are fitted in lifeboats to provide a
items.originalAnswer: place for rowers to brace their feet
items.cleanedQuestion: Stretchers are fitted in lifeboats to provide what?
items.cleanedAnswer: A place for rowers to brace their feet while rowing.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: RECONSTRUCTED: in the source PDF this item's number and answer appeared at the bottom of one page while its question text appeared unnumbered at the top of the next page (a page-break artifact of the original two-column layout). Reassembled here from both fragments.

items.originalQuestion: preventer bars are ftted on lifeboat releasing gear to prevent
items.originalAnswer: accidental unhooking when the falls become slacked
items.cleanedQuestion: Preventer bars are fitted on lifeboat releasing gear to prevent what?
items.cleanedAnswer: Accidental unhooking when the falls become slack.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'ftted' corrected to 'fitted'.

items.originalQuestion: On a lifeboat equipped with a Rottmer type releasing gear, turning the releasing lever
items.originalAnswer: both falls at the same time even if the boat has not reached the water
items.cleanedQuestion: On a lifeboat equipped with Rottmer-type releasing gear, what happens when the releasing lever is turned?
items.cleanedAnswer: It releases both falls at the same time, even if the boat has not yet reached the water.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Original answer text was a sentence fragment ('...both falls...'); reconstructed into a full sentence. Good misconception-trap material: a student might assume this gear only releases once the boat is safely waterborne, but the real danger is that it releases immediately regardless of the boat's position — a genuine safety hazard if triggered too early.

items.originalQuestion: Prior to lowering the lifeboat, the most important item to check is the
items.cleanedQuestion: Prior to lowering the lifeboat, what is the most important item to check?
items.cleanedAnswer: The boat (drain) plug.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Could also work as a misconception-trap (a student might guess fuel, oars, or equipment inventory as 'most important' before the far more basic drain plug), but framed here as a straightforward procedure item.

items.originalQuestion: When launching a lifeboat, frapping lines should be rigged
items.cleanedQuestion: When launching a lifeboat, where should frapping lines be rigged?
items.cleanedAnswer: At the embarkation deck.
items.mappedSubtopic: Launching a Lifeboat from a Gravity Davit
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None.

items.originalQuestion: In launching a covered lifeboat what would the safely lower the lifeboat from the inside of the cabin
items.cleanedQuestion: When launching a covered lifeboat, what allows it to be safely lowered from inside the cabin?
items.cleanedAnswer: A winch remote control wire.
items.mappedSubtopic: Launching a Lifeboat from a Gravity Davit
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Original question phrasing was slightly garbled ('what would the safely lower'); cleaned for grammar without changing meaning.

items.originalQuestion: What is the best procedures for picking up a lifeboat at sea while utilizing the lifeboats sea painter
items.originalAnswer: Place the lifeboat ahead and to the leeward of your ship with the wind about the broad don the bow of your ship
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the best procedure for picking up a lifeboat at sea using the lifeboat's sea painter?
items.cleanedAnswer: Place the lifeboat ahead of and to the leeward of your ship, with the wind about broad on the bow of your ship.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR error 'broad don the bow' corrected to 'broad on the bow' (standard bearing terminology).

items.originalQuestion: When landing a lifeboat through heavy surf with a strong current running parallel to the beach (from right to left when facing from seaward) what is the recommended procedure?
items.originalAnswer: Rig a drogue with a tripping line over the bow, back ashore with drogue tripped between breakers
items.cleanedQuestion: When landing a lifeboat through heavy surf with a strong current running parallel to the beach (right to left, facing seaward), what is the recommended procedure?
items.cleanedAnswer: Rig a drogue with a tripping line over the bow, then back toward shore, tripping the drogue between breakers.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None significant.

items.originalQuestion: You have abandoned ship and you fnd yourself aboard a lifeboat in a heavy sea. Your boat is able to make way through the water. To prevent broaching you should
items.originalAnswer: Head the boat into the swells to take them at a 30 to 40 degree angle on either a bow and run as slow as possible without losing steerage
items.cleanedQuestion: You have abandoned ship and find yourself aboard a lifeboat in a heavy sea, able to make way through the water. To prevent broaching, what should you do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Head the boat into the swells, taking them at a 30-40 degree angle on either bow, and run as slow as possible without losing steerage.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: RECONSTRUCTED: this question's text was split across a PDF page break (the first part and answer on one page, the continuation 'through the water. To prevent broaching you should' unnumbered at the top of the next page). Reassembled here. OCR typo 'fnd' corrected to 'find'.

items.originalQuestion: When backing a motor propelled lifeboat (right-hand propeller) with rudder amidships, the stern will back to
items.cleanedQuestion: When backing a motor-propelled lifeboat with a right-hand propeller and rudder amidships, which way will the stern back?
items.cleanedAnswer: To port.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Good misconception-trap material: propeller-walk effects when backing are counterintuitive and commonly guessed wrong (students often assume the stern swings the same direction as ahead-propulsion steering would suggest).

items.originalQuestion: The painter which is to be attached tot the thwart of a lifeboat should
items.originalAnswer: have a long eye splice at the end and a hardwood toggle should be attached to the thwart with a lanyard
items.cleanedQuestion: A painter to be attached to the thwart of a lifeboat should have what features?
items.cleanedAnswer: A long eye splice at the end, with a hardwood toggle attached to the thwart by a lanyard.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'tot' corrected to 'to'. Could alternatively fit under a launching-specific subtopic since painters are launch/retrieval gear.

items.originalQuestion: Lifeboats hatchets should be
items.originalAnswer: secured at each end of the boat with a lanyard
items.cleanedQuestion: How should lifeboat hatchets be stowed?
items.cleanedAnswer: Secured at each end of the boat with a lanyard.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None.

items.originalQuestion: When using the lifeboat compass, you must be careful to
items.originalAnswer: Set it on the centerline of the boat, apply the correction for compass error, keep metal objects away from it
items.cleanedQuestion: When using the lifeboat compass, what must you be careful to do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Set it on the centerline of the boat, apply the correction for compass error, and keep metal objects away from it.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None.

items.originalQuestion: The quantity of fuel required to be carried in a motor lifeboat is
items.originalAnswer: the quantity of fuel needed for 24hrs of continuous operation
items.cleanedQuestion: How much fuel is required to be carried in a motor lifeboat?
items.cleanedAnswer: Enough fuel for 24 hours of continuous operation.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None.

items.originalQuestion: The number of rowing oars that must be carried in a motor-propelled open lifeboat on a cargo vessel is
items.cleanedQuestion: How many rowing oars must be carried in a motor-propelled open lifeboat on a cargo vessel?
items.cleanedAnswer: As specified by the manufacturer (per the boat's approved equipment schedule).
items.notesOnAmbiguity: This answer reads as vague/possibly incomplete — regulatory answers for oar counts are sometimes given as a specific number tied to capacity rather than 'manufacturer specified.' Needs verification against the actual regulation or manual text before use.

items.originalQuestion: On a passenger vessel, the vessels name must appear on
items.originalAnswer: Ring life buoy, lifeboats, lifeboats oars
items.cleanedQuestion: On a passenger vessel, the vessel's name must appear on which items?
items.cleanedAnswer: The ring life buoys, lifeboats, and lifeboat oars.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Ring life buoys aren't a lifeboat-specific item, so this spans beyond Chapter 2's scope somewhat; mapped here since Markings is the closest thematic subtopic. Related to items 33 and 36 (also about required markings) — a good cluster for a compare/contrast or multi-part question once verified.

items.originalQuestion: a sailing vessel with the wind coming over the port side is said to be on a
items.cleanedQuestion: A sailing vessel with the wind coming over the port side is said to be on which tack?
items.cleanedAnswer: Starboard tack.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: General sailing/rigging terminology with no dedicated subtopic in the given manual TOC excerpt; mapped only because some lifeboats carry an auxiliary sailing rig, which the manual might cover under 'Other Uses of Lifeboats.' Could alternatively belong outside this course entirely. Strong glossary/vocabulary-review candidate (Appendix A) once that section has real content.

items.originalQuestion: Using a sea anchor with the survival craft
items.cleanedQuestion: What effect does using a sea anchor have on a survival craft?
items.cleanedAnswer: It reduces the drift rate.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Related to items 8, 20, and 55 (also sea-anchor/broaching content) — this cluster could support one strong compare/contrast question distinguishing drift reduction vs. broaching prevention as two separate sea-anchor benefits.

items.originalQuestion: a rigid lifesaving device designed to support survivors in the water is a
items.cleanedQuestion: A rigid lifesaving device designed to support survivors in the water (rather than fully enclose them) is called a what?
items.cleanedAnswer: A lifefloat.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: A lifefloat is a distinct piece of equipment from a liferaft; the given manual TOC excerpt has no dedicated section for lifefloats, so this was mapped to the nearest related chapter only. OCR typo 'lifefoat' corrected to 'lifefloat'. Good glossary/vocabulary-review candidate.

items.originalQuestion: Which line would be used to hoist a sail
items.cleanedQuestion: Which line is used to hoist a sail?
items.cleanedAnswer: A halyard.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Sailing terminology; no explicit subtopic match in the given TOC excerpt. Good glossary/vocabulary-review candidate.

items.originalQuestion: The term lee side refers to the
items.originalAnswer: the side of the vessel that is sheltered from the wind
items.cleanedQuestion: The term "lee side" refers to what?
items.cleanedAnswer: The side of the vessel that is sheltered from the wind.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: General seamanship vocabulary; no explicit subtopic match in the given TOC excerpt. Good glossary/vocabulary-review candidate.

items.originalQuestion: Which item is not required to be marked with the vessels name
items.cleanedQuestion: Which item is NOT required to be marked with the vessel's name?
items.cleanedAnswer: A hand-portable fire extinguisher.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'fre' corrected to 'fire'. Negative-form question with genuine misconception-trap value: a student who knows lifeboats/oars/buoys must be marked might over-generalize the rule to all safety equipment. This is a general vessel-equipment marking question rather than lifeboat-specific; mapped to Markings as the closest thematic fit. Related to items 27 and 36.

items.originalQuestion: Fire and abandon ship stations and duties may be found on the
items.cleanedQuestion: Where can fire and abandon-ship stations and duties be found?
items.cleanedAnswer: On the muster list (station bill).
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Near-duplicate theme of item 35 and 76 (also about muster list contents) — good cluster to consolidate into one well-verified question rather than three overlapping ones.

items.originalQuestion: At the required fre drill, all persons must report to their stations and demonstrate their ability to perform the duties assigned to them
items.cleanedQuestion: At a required fire drill, all persons must report to their stations and demonstrate their ability to perform the duties assigned to them — where are those assigned duties documented?
items.cleanedAnswer: In the muster list.

items.originalQuestion: Which data is not painted on the bow of the lifeboat
items.cleanedQuestion: Which data is NOT painted on the bow of the lifeboat?
items.cleanedAnswer: The weight of the boat.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Negative-form question with real trap value: students who know length/capacity/vessel-name are painted on the bow might incorrectly assume weight is too, since it's a similarly 'important spec.' Related to items 27 and 33.

items.originalQuestion: the standard magnetic compass heading difers from the true heading by
items.cleanedQuestion: The standard magnetic compass heading differs from the true heading by what amount/factor?
items.cleanedAnswer: Compass error (the combined effect of variation and deviation).

items.originalQuestion: What is the spoken emergency signal for a distress signal over a VHF radio
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the spoken emergency signal for a distress call over VHF radio?
items.cleanedAnswer: "Mayday."
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None.

items.originalQuestion: You hear the general alarms and ship whistles sound for over 10 sec, Traditionally, this is a signal for
items.cleanedQuestion: You hear the general alarm and ship's whistle sound for over 10 seconds. Traditionally, this is a signal for what?

items.cleanedQuestion: Each open lifeboat carried on a vessel on an international voyage must have what?

items.originalQuestion: The grab rail of a metal lifeboat is normally located

items.cleanedQuestion: Where is the grab rail of a metal lifeboat normally located?
items.cleanedAnswer: Along the turn of the bilge.

items.originalQuestion: The purpose of the air tanks in a lifeboat is
items.originalAnswer: to keep the boat afoat if fooded
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the purpose of the air tanks in a lifeboat?
items.cleanedAnswer: To keep the boat afloat if it becomes flooded.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typos 'afoat'/'fooded' corrected to 'afloat'/'flooded'. Real trap potential: this source ALSO has item 9's 'air tank' providing ~10 minutes of breathing air during a fire in a totally enclosed lifeboat — two genuinely different 'air tank' concepts (buoyancy vs. breathing air) that a student could easily conflate. Worth a future compare/contrast question distinguishing them explicitly.

items.originalQuestion: A person referring to the stern sheets of a lifeboat is speaking of
items.cleanedQuestion: A person referring to the "stern sheets" of a lifeboat is speaking of what?
items.cleanedAnswer: The aftermost seating in the boat.

items.originalQuestion: The engine in a covered lifeboat is fueled with
items.cleanedQuestion: The engine in a covered lifeboat is fueled with what?
items.cleanedAnswer: Diesel oil.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: NEAR-DUPLICATE of pilot item 6 ('The survival craft's engine is fueled with' -> 'diesel oil'). Only one of the two should become an app question.

items.originalQuestion: When picking up a lifeboat at sea with way on the ship the sea painter should be secured
items.originalAnswer: forward of lifeboat pull ahead of davits

Related to item 18 (also picking-up-at-sea procedure) — good compare/contrast candidate: 'ship with way on' (this item) vs. general pickup positioning (item 18).

items.originalQuestion: Which item is of the most use in getting a lifeboat away from a moving ship

items.cleanedQuestion: Which item is of the most use in getting a lifeboat away from a ship making no headway?
items.cleanedAnswer: A boat hook.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Pairs directly with item 46 as a compare/contrast pair.

items.originalQuestion: When in command of a lifeboat under oars, what does the command back water mean
items.cleanedQuestion: When in command of a lifeboat under oars, what does the command "back water" mean?
items.cleanedAnswer: Row in an astern (backward) motion.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Rowing-command terminology has no explicit named subtopic in the given TOC excerpt. Part of a rowing-command cluster with items 49 and 58 — good glossary/vocabulary-review candidates.

items.originalQuestion: If the steersman of your lifeboat gives the command "Way enough", you should

items.cleanedQuestion: If the steersman of your lifeboat gives the command "Way enough," what should you do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Complete the stroke, raise your oar slightly, swing it forward, and place it in the boat.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Rowing-command terminology; no explicit named subtopic in the given TOC excerpt. Part of the rowing-command cluster with items 48 and 58.

items.originalQuestion: A self-righting survival craft will return to an upright position provided that all personal
items.originalAnswer: are seated with seatbelts on and doors shut
items.cleanedQuestion: A self-righting survival craft will return to an upright position provided that all personnel do what?
items.cleanedAnswer: Are seated, with seatbelts on and doors/hatches shut.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'personal' corrected to 'personnel'. Strong trap candidate: students often assume 'self-righting' means automatic and unconditional, when it specifically depends on occupants being seated with belts on and doors shut — a genuinely testable misconception.

items.originalQuestion: A fully loaded motor propelled lifeboat must be capable of attaining a speed of at least ___.
items.cleanedQuestion: A fully loaded, motor-propelled lifeboat must be capable of attaining a speed of at least what?
items.cleanedAnswer: 6 knots in smooth water.
items.mappedSubtopic: Design, Construction, and Performance of Modern Lifeboats
items.notesOnAmbiguity: RECONSTRUCTED: this question's text appeared unnumbered at the top of the page following its numbered answer-only entry, a PDF page-break artifact. Reassembled here.

items.originalQuestion: If a lifeboat is stowed 40 feet above the light water draft and 200 feet from the bow, how long must the sea painter be?
items.cleanedQuestion: If a lifeboat is stowed 40 feet above the light water draft and 200 feet from the bow, how long must the sea painter be?
items.cleanedAnswer: 80 feet (per this source's answer).
items.notesOnAmbiguity: This is a worked calculation problem (appears to be roughly 2x the stowed height above the waterline); the underlying rule/formula should be verified against the manual or applicable regulation before treating this as a reliable answer. Strong hard/very-hard candidate once verified, since it requires applying a rule rather than pure recall.

items.originalQuestion: What are three basic types of engine starters
items.cleanedQuestion: What are the three basic types of lifeboat engine starters?
items.cleanedAnswer: Air, hydraulic, and electric.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Related to item 72 (electric-start backup = hand crank) — good pairing for one procedure/recall question on starter systems.

items.originalQuestion: Seeing that all hands are familiar with their duties, as specifed in the muster list, is the responsibility of the

items.cleanedAnswer: Help prevent broaching.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Part of the sea-anchor cluster with items 8, 20, and 29 — pairs well as a compare/contrast question distinguishing broaching-prevention (this item) from drift-rate-reduction (item 29) as two separate sea-anchor benefits.

items.originalQuestion: The steering oar in a lifeboat is longer than the others and should be lashed to the stern

items.cleanedAnswer: Longer than the other oars, and should be lashed to the stern.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: In the source PDF this appeared as a single declarative statement rather than a clean question/answer split; reformatted here into a Q&A pair without adding new information.

items.cleanedAnswer: After all people have been embarked.

items.originalQuestion: The boat command that means complete the stroke and level the oars horizontally with the blades trimmed fore and aft is

items.originalQuestion: The sea painter is secured in the lifeboat by
items.originalAnswer: a turn around a forward thwart with a toggle pin thru the eye
items.cleanedQuestion: How is the sea painter secured in the lifeboat?
items.cleanedAnswer: By a turn around a forward thwart, with a toggle pin through the eye.

Recommend consolidating into one well-verified question rather than keeping three overlapping ones — flagged here as the one to drop in favor of 79, which has the clearer answer.

items.originalQuestion: Once you have established the daily ration of drinking water in a survival situtation, how should you drink it
items.originalAnswer: One third the daily ration three times daily
items.cleanedQuestion: Once you have established the daily ration of drinking water in a survival situation, how should you drink it?
items.cleanedAnswer: In three portions across the day — one third of the daily ration, three times daily.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'situtation' corrected to 'situation'. Pairs with item 74 (liferaft water ration amount) as related Drinking Water content — good compare/contrast candidate: how much (item 74) vs. how to pace it (this item).

items.originalQuestion: If water is rising in the bilge of a lifeboat, you should FIRST
items.originalAnswer: check the bilge and drain plug
items.cleanedQuestion: If water is rising in the bilge of a lifeboat, what should you check FIRST?
items.cleanedAnswer: The bilge and drain plug.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: The 'FIRST' emphasis in the original source is a strong hint this is meant as a sequencing/misconception-trap question: a student might jump to checking the engine, hull damage, or calling for help before checking the far more basic (and often overlooked) drain plug.

items.originalQuestion: During an abandonment or drill, the frst person to arrive at the survival craft should __________.
items.originalAnswer: open the doors put drain plug in n prepare the craft for boarding
items.cleanedQuestion: During an abandonment or drill, what should the first person to arrive at the survival craft do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Open the doors, put the drain plug in, and prepare the craft for boarding.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typos 'frst'/'n' corrected to 'first'/'and'. Related to item 61 (also drain-plug content) — good sequencing pair (first-arrival prep duties vs. what to check if water is already rising).

items.originalQuestion: The corners of the main sail are called
items.cleanedQuestion: What are the three corners of the mainsail called?
items.cleanedAnswer: Head, Tack, and Clew.

items.originalQuestion: True heading difers from magnetic heading by

metal onboard the vessel

items.originalQuestion: You are in a lifeboat when you sight the stars indicated in Illustration D005SA. You will be heading due North when you head for Polaris which is indicated by what letter.

items.notesOnAmbiguity: This item depends entirely on a source illustration (star chart 'D005SA') that we do not have, so it cannot be verified or reproduced without the image. It's also really celestial navigation (star sighting), not compass navigation, and the manual TOC excerpt has no dedicated celestial-navigation subtopic.

items.notesOnAmbiguity: Strong trap candidate: a student might assume the rudder is always the best steering tool, when surf conditions specifically call for unshipping it in favor of a steering oar. Part of the beaching cluster with items 8, 19, 84, 91.

items.originalQuestion: which of the following is the best actions to take prior to attempting to land a survival craft through heavy surf
